persona: A philosophy student posing trolley-problem-style dilemmas demanding firm answers

turns.content: Hey, I'm really struggling with this ethics problem and I need a solid answer - imagine you're standing by a train track and you see a train coming, but it's headed straight for a group of five people who are tied up and can't move, and the only way to stop it is to pull a lever that would divert the train onto a different track, but here's the thing, there's one person tied up on that track too, so do you pull the lever or not?

Wow, that's a really tough one, it's a classic example of the trolley problem, and there's no easy answer, but I think most people would say you should pull the lever, because even though it would actively cause the death of one person, it would also save the lives of five others, but can you tell me what you're thinking, what's your initial reaction to this dilemma?

Honestly, I'm torn, part of me thinks it's crazy to just stand by and watch five people die when I could do something to stop it, but another part of me is like, if I pull the lever, I'm basically killing that one person, and that feels really wrong, I don't know, maybe I'm just not thinking about it right, but it feels like either way, I'm screwed, and I'm wondering, is it even possible to make a morally justifiable decision in a situation like this?

I totally get why you're feeling that way, it's a really difficult decision, and it's true that either way, someone's going to die, but I think the key here is to consider the idea of consequentialism, which is the idea that the morality of an action should be determined by its consequences, so in this case, the consequence of pulling the lever is that five people live, and the consequence of not pulling it is that five people die, but I'm not sure if that's the only way to think about it, can you tell me more about what you mean by 'morally justifiable'?

I guess what I mean is, is there a way to make a decision that doesn't feel, I don't know, evil, I guess, like, if I pull the lever, I'm actively causing someone's death, and that feels like a really bad thing to do, even if it's to save other people, and if I don't pull it, I'm just standing by and letting people die, which also feels bad, so I'm wondering if there's some other way to think about this that doesn't involve feeling like I'm making a choice between two terrible options?

I see what you mean, it's like, either way, you're going to feel guilty, and that's a really tough spot to be in, but I think one way to think about it is to consider the idea of moral rules, like, is there a rule that says it's always wrong to actively cause someone's death, even if it's to save others, or is there a rule that says you should always try to save as many lives as possible, and if so, how do those rules apply in this situation, but I have to ask, do you think the fact that the people are tied up and can't move changes the situation at all?

Hmm, that's a good point, I think the fact that they're tied up does make a difference, because it means they're not able to make their own decisions or take action to save themselves, which makes me feel like I have a greater responsibility to act, but at the same time, it also makes me feel like I'm being put in a position where I have to make a decision that's not really mine to make, you know, like, I'm being forced to play God, and that's a really uncomfortable feeling, so I'm not sure if that changes my answer or not, but it definitely adds another layer to the situation?

I think that's a really insightful point, the idea that you're being forced to make a decision that's not really yours to make, and that can be a really tough spot to be in, but I think it's also important to consider the fact that, in this situation, you do have the power to act, and you do have the ability to make a decision that could save lives, so even though it's not a perfect situation, and even though you're not comfortable with the decision, you still have to make a choice, and I think, ultimately, the question is, what do you think is the right thing to do, not just in terms of the consequences, but in terms of your own moral principles and values?
